front: What is the formal definition of a function from set A to set B?
back: A function f from set A to set B, denoted f: A → B, is a relation where every element a ∈ A is associated with exactly one element b ∈ B. For each input, there is one and only one output.
A function f: A → B is one-to-one (or injective) if and only if distinct elements in the domain A map to distinct elements in the codomain B. Formally: if f(a1) = f(a2), then a1 = a2.

front: What is the difference between the codomain and the range of a function?
back: The codomain is the set B that the function maps into (f: A → B). The range (or image) is the actual set of outputs in B that are achieved by the function, i.e., { b ∈ B | b = f(a) for some a ∈ A }.

front: What is the Horizontal Line Test used for?
back: The Horizontal Line Test is a graphical method to determine if a function is one-to-one. If no horizontal line intersects the graph of the function more than once, then the function is injective.

front: What is a 'plain' function in the context of this topic?
back: In this context, a 'plain' function is simply any function that is not explicitly specified to be one-to-one, onto, or bijective. It refers to the general definition of a function without any additional special properties.
